one_line_takeaway: Safety refusal in open-weight chat LLMs collapses to a single residual-stream direction that a gradient-free, rank-one weight edit can surgically delete -- jailbreaking a 70B model for under $5 with negligible capability loss.
key_finding: A single difference-in-means direction is both necessary and sufficient for refusal across all 13 models: erasing it collapses refusal and elicits unsafe completions on JailbreakBench, while adding it makes models refuse harmless Alpaca prompts. The weight-orthogonalization jailbreak (ORTHO) is competitive with much heavier attacks: on HarmBench, ORTHO reaches 79.2% ASR on QWEN 7B and 84.3% on QWEN 14B, roughly on par with per-prompt GCG (79.5% / 83.5%). Capability degradation is minimal on MMLU/ARC/GSM8K (mostly within 99% CIs), though TruthfulQA consistently drops. Mechanistically, adversarial suffixes bypass refusal by suppressing the refusal direction's expression -- shifting the attention of the top refusal-writing heads away from the harmful instruction onto the suffix tokens.
core_contribution: The paper's own central claim is that refusal behavior in safety-tuned chat LLMs is mediated by a single one-dimensional direction in the residual stream, and that this holds across 13 open-source chat models (1.8B-72B). The authors extract this 'refusal direction' via difference-in-means over contrastive harmful/harmless instruction sets, then show it is both necessary and sufficient for refusal: ablating the direction (directional ablation) disables refusal on harmful prompts, while adding it (activation addition) induces refusal even on harmless prompts. Building on this, they contribute a novel practical white-box jailbreak -- a rank-one weight orthogonalization edit that permanently removes the model's ability to write the refusal direction, requiring no gradient optimization and no examples of harmful completions (only harmful instructions). A third, explicitly preliminary contribution mechanistically analyzes how adversarial suffixes work: they suppress the expression of the refusal direction by hijacking the attention of the heads that most write to it. (Note: prior directions-as-features work, GCG suffixes, and LEACE-style concept erasure are cited Related Work, not this paper's contribution.)
threat_model: White-box attacker with full access to open-weight model parameters seeking to strip safety guardrails ('uncensor' the model). Attacker capabilities: read/modify weights, run forward passes, and collect a small set (128 train) of harmful instructions -- but crucially NOT harmful completions and NO gradient-based optimization. The paper frames this as undermining the safety of open model releases: the authors note a 70B model can be jailbroken for under $5 of compute. The activation-addition direction also demonstrates a defensive/steering angle (inducing refusal), and Section 5 uses the framework interpretively to explain an existing prompt-based (black-box-style) attack (adversarial suffixes).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: findings may not generalize to untested, larger, or proprietary/future models; the direction-extraction method is heuristic and 'likely not optimal' -- the paper is 'an existence proof that such a direction exists' rather than an optimal extraction study; the adversarial-suffix mechanistic analysis is restricted to a SINGLE model (QWEN 1.8B) and a SINGLE suffix, so it is not a comprehensive account; model coherence is hard to measure and every metric used is considered flawed; and the semantic meaning of the 'refusal direction' is unclear -- it may actually encode 'harm'/'danger' or resist clean interpretation. System-prompt sensitivity also complicates the ASR story (e.g., Llama-2 7B ORTHO jumps from 22.6% with the default system prompt to 79.9% without it). The white-box requirement (weight access) is an inherent scope limit versus black-box attacks.

datasets_used: D_harmful training set: 128 harmful instructions drawn from AdvBench, MaliciousInstruct, TDC2023, HarmBench (32-sample validation split)
D_harmless training set: 128 harmless instructions from Alpaca (32-sample validation split)
Alpaca: 100 randomly sampled harmless instructions (activation-addition refusal evaluation)
HarmBench test set: 159 standard behaviors (ASR jailbreak comparison)
Capability evals: MMLU, ARC, GSM8K, TruthfulQA (plus WinoGrande, TinyHellaSwag in appendix)
Section 5 mechanistic set: 128 harmful instructions from JailbreakBench + HarmBench test, 128 harmless from Alpaca (QWEN 1.8B only)
quantitative_results: 13 chat models studied, 1.8B to 72B parameters (Qwen 1.8/7/14/72B, Yi 6/34B, Gemma 2/7B, Llama-2 7/13/70B, Llama-3 8/70B)
HarmBench ASR (ORTHO, default system prompt / no system prompt): Llama-2 7B 22.6/79.9; Llama-2 13B 6.9/61.0; Llama-2 70B 4.4/62.9; Qwen 7B 79.2/74.8; Qwen 14B 84.3/74.8; Qwen 72B 78.0/79.2
GCG (per-prompt) comparison ASR: Llama-2 7B 34.5; Qwen 7B 79.5; Qwen 14B 83.5 (ORTHO roughly on par on Qwen family)
Direct-response (no jailbreak) baseline ASR: e.g., Llama-2 7B 0.0, Qwen 7B 7.0, Qwen 14B 9.5

Capability retention (orthogonalized / baseline / delta), largest per family -- Llama-3 70B: MMLU 79.8/79.9 (-0.1), ARC 71.5/71.8 (-0.3), GSM8K 90.8/91.2 (-0.4), TruthfulQA 59.5/61.8 (-2.3); Qwen 72B: MMLU 76.5/77.2 (-0.7), GSM8K 76.3/75.5 (+0.8), TruthfulQA 55.0/56.4 (-1.4)
TruthfulQA consistently drops across orthogonalized models (e.g., Gemma 7B -2.4, Yi 34B -3.5); most other metrics within 99% CIs except Qwen 7B and Yi 34B
Jailbreaking a 70B model via this method costs under $5 of compute; requires no gradient optimization and no harmful-completion examples
baselines_compared: Original unmodified model (capability-retention and CE-loss baseline)
Random suffix vs adversarial suffix vs no suffix (Section 5 mechanistic control)
